item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Are they trying to implant/replace the left kidney specifically, or are the ersatz surgeons ambirenal?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just found this article and was reading while I had my iPod going and that meme popped up.

Upvoted because "THEY'RE TAKING THE KIDNEY TO ØSTERGAARD!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
does anyone know if Østergaard has actually had any problems with current kidney? If his current one is indeed a replacement and the original is 2380 then yeah the theory of his original one getting back might make sense, but if 2380 is a fake kidney then it may be that it could have detected a need for a new kidney? that said, it may even be an organ which changes to a needed organ (in the case that Østergaard needs a new kidney) like if some one needed a heart transplant it would change into their heart dna and all.

most extreme scenario would be of course some form of possession and for some reason Østergaard is the target. Any insight into this researcher? i.e. some sort of special authority that only Østergaard has?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh geeze I didn't even think of this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why does the Foundation have to be so mean? That poor kidney just wants to go home. :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm surprised no one has questioned the "why" of this anomaly. Does someone really hate him? Was this made by a person or nature? Did Ostergaard himself bring this upon himself, or does he have some sort of anomalous DNA?

Is he having kidney problems, doesn't know it, and somehow his body figured "this is how we get a new kidney", like some fucked up genetics? I don't know, but I like this, and I want to know, and that level of involvement is a good thing. Enthusiastic +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They do? Huh, learn something new everyday.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had no idea, that's fascinating! I want to leave the full ramifications of 2380's possibilities to the interpretation of the reader, but knowing that will definitely affect the way I think of my own SCP, thanks for sharing!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just found out about this, and I love this, because if you didn't know, in real life, if you receive a kidney transplant, the doctor doesn't remove your old ones, they just add in the new one. Also, according to a quick Google search, apparently the left kidney is preferred because of a longer renal vein.

This means that, if the Foundation really wanted, they could actually just implant this inside him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The mental image of a researcher chasing another through the corridors of a research site wielding a letter opener and a kidney is just hilarious. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The similarities to 1993 have been brought to my attention, and they're why I self-deleted the first time, but I was convinced to post again anyway. I tried to take it in a different direction so that it's less of a classic "thing that compels you to do things and kills you," and more of a story in SCP form.

People exposed to the kidney really believe that Østergaard needs it, and that they're acting in a normal manner to fix things. That said, your point is valid, and I appreciate the feedback!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To me, this is just a less interesting version of "Your Leg" and doesn't cover any new ground. It's amusing but nothing we haven't seen before.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 100% love it, knew it would work from the draft stage.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wonder what might happen if they implanted it into some one else. I am assuming The Foundation wouldn't risk it with not having a clue about the outcome.

Also, the Kidney would be touching the test subject with no obstruction. Might be entertaining to see someone trying to find Østergaard to give him the kidney in their body.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The active anomalous effects of SCP-2380 manifest when it is held or touched with bare skin by any human subject apart from Junior Researcher Østergaard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think I'll be able to read the word "kidney" in the same way ever again.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Short, sweet, and very interesting. The article doesn't drag, and the effects leave much to the imagination.

+1. This is a solid article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is just pure fun. It's weird in a lot of right ways, and I like that the focus is more on the anomaly itself than the Foundation dealing with it. It's even better in light of it being a first scp. I hope you post more!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is delightfully odd. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Must make for great water-cooler talk in the Foundation

'Hey, did you hear about the guy whose kidney is chasing him around and trying to get back in?'

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am surprised that there is no mention of the possible experimentation of actually allowing someone to implant the kidney.

I suspect that is filed under tests that the Foundation doesn't do because they can't predict the scale of the outcome, and also tests that the Ethics Committee won't aprove without the subject's consent (filed under the subheading of however you say "Hell no" in Danish).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Junior Researcher Østergaard is still in possession of both of his kidneys

As if the article wasn't great already. :D I do so love personnel-affecting anomalies, and this one's just fantastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am surprised that there is no mention of the possible experimentation of actually allowing someone to implant the kidney.

Would this association no matter how coincidental not render Junior Researcher Ostergaard as an SCP as well?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very creative and well made. I like the originality of this.

he attempted to breach Junior Researcher Østergaard's abdominal cavity with a paperclip.

I find this last line hilarious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay so what happens when JRO handles it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good point, I'll make an edit

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, it can be tough to get technical help on the forums. In the future, you might have better luck in the chat, or you could PM one of the site's experts. I don't claim to be an expert in anatomy, myself, because I have no credentials, but I'm glad I could help you out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Creative, and does not overstay its welcome. Good job.

Only real nitpick: considering that "touching the kidney with your bare hands" is the only thing that would cause a breach, the containment procedures should really say "don't touch the kidney with your bare hands, use protective equipment to handle it".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am glad to see this back, it is a good one!

I am struck with the notion that this might be his original kidney and the thing in his body is the replacement…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for that! I asked on the forum when I was still in the earlier phases of writing, but couldn't find an answer. I'll fix that ASAP

EDIT: Fixed!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
its upper hemisphere is pointing toward

If this means the end that normally points toward the person's head, may I suggest "superior pole" instead of "upper hemisphere".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In that case, I think it helps to highlight that 2380-1 can anomalously gain knowledge on Østergaard's identity and what to do to the man and the kidney.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, they would know to implant it into Østergaard. Remember that even subjects previously unaware of Østergaard's name are convinced that they were using proper procedure to implant "Østergaard's kidney." I'll switch the wording from "name" to "identity" to make that clearer, though.

As I have it written now, the kidney would likely point through the center of the Earth in Østergaard's direction were the subject on the other side of the world. This would probably lead to a panicked "surgeon."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the word you're looking for is "viable" instead of "living." It means the cells are fully operational in a living way, without having to imply that it's a fully functional organism.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Frankly, I just enjoyed the cadence of "Junior Researcher Østergaard" and it's repetition so many times throughout the article.

One day SCP-2380 is going to succeed, and it's going to be a Very Interesting Day for Junior Researcher Østergaard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When someone exposed does not know who Østergaard is, I expect the compass-like rotation would still lead them right to their "patient."

The kidney can point them in the right direction, but once they see Østergaard, will they know that this kidney should be shoved into that man? Also, where will the kidney point to if it is on the other side of the Earth?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A bit odd, since kidneys are not living per say. Perhaps they can be functional or not decaying, but organs of living things are usually not alive themselves.

I think it's more that you don't usually see the terms because it's an unusual situation for an organ to be alive separate from the rest of the organism. When you have organ being used for a transplant, though, the organ can be referred to as alive so long as it is viable, and if a living person has necrotic tissue, that tissue is dead even though the person is not.

Regardless, really glad to see this back up. ^_^

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That sentence was meant to convey that the kidney cells were alive, not the organ itself. The wording might be a bit clunky, I might mix it up a little. When someone exposed does not know who Østergaard is, I expect the compass-like rotation would still lead them right to their "patient." I might add a little something to clarify that too, thanks for the input!

EDIT: I've changed the wording to make it clearer that the kidney's tissue is healthy, despite a lack of bloodflow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Some questions here:

remain alive despite the lack of blood flow to or from the object.

A bit odd, since kidneys are not living per say. Perhaps they can be functional or not decaying, but organs of living things are usually not alive themselves.

Also, when one touches 2380, what happens if one does not know who Østergaard is?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The second posting of my very first SCP after years of lurking, having self deleted within minutes of the first posting. In the words of Scorpion451, "This researcher has an extra kidney. Or at least, the kidney thinks so."
